ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for maitake stir-fry with garlic. Mapped to closest parent mushroom food product class (FOODON_03302596) as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for maitake stir-fry. A00LC (mushrooms and fungi products) used as the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for maitake stir-fry dish. Not mapped to avoid fabrication.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and Shaoxing wine)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of maitake mushroom stir-fry with garlic is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a side dish alongside steamed rice or other main courses in a Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Mushrooms, maitake, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403); adapted for stir-fried preparation with garlic, soy sauce, and oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw maitake mushroom values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169403) used as the primary nutritional anchor. Values adjusted upward for sodium (cooking oil addition ~4.5 g/100g), and minor carbohydrate contribution from Shaoxing wine and soy sauce. Energy recalculated accordingly. Saturated fat estimated at ~0.6 g/100g based on typical vegetable oil composition. All adjustments documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms and low-carbohydrate vegetables — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for maitake stir-fry with garlic. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy vegetables and mushrooms as very low GI foods (GI < 20). Maitake mushrooms are predominantly water, dietary fibre, and beta-glucan polysaccharides with minimal digestible starch. The small carbohydrate contribution from soy sauce and Shaoxing wine is insufficient to materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.8 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 1.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce (~210 mg/100g); partially offset by high potassium (320 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the sodium contributed by soy sauce in this dish (~315 mg per 150 g serving); using reduced-sodium soy sauce and limiting portion size can help maintain sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day threshold, while the high potassium content from maitake mushrooms supports vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load (~1.5 per serving); beta-glucan polysaccharides in maitake may support postprandial glucose regulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (estimated 15), low glycemic load (~1.5 per 150 g serving), and high dietary fibre content (2.5 g/100g); the beta-glucan and D-fraction polysaccharides in maitake mushrooms have been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in preclinical studies, though clinical evidence in humans remains limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100g), cholesterol-free, high potassium, and anti-inflammatory compounds from maitake and garlic
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with low saturated fat (~0.9 g per serving), zero cholesterol, and a high potassium-to-sodium ratio that supports blood pressure regulation; garlic's allicin compounds and maitake's beta-glucans have demonstrated lipid-lowering and anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed research, making this dish a heart-healthy choice when soy sauce sodium is moderated.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polysaccharides in maitake and allicin in garlic
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Both maitake mushrooms and garlic contain bioactive compounds with documented anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating properties, making this dish a suitable dietary choice for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maitake Mushroom Stir-fry with Garlic (蒜香舞茸, Suàn Xiāng Wǔ Róng) is a Chinese vegetable side dish featuring Grifola frondosa — the maitake or 'Dancing Mushroom' — wok-tossed at high heat with smashed garlic, light soy sauce, and a splash of Shaoxing rice wine. The mushrooms are torn into feathery bite-sized clusters before cooking, which allows the edges to caramelise and crisp slightly while the interior remains tender and succulent. The flavour profile is deeply savoury and umami-rich, with the pungent sweetness of garlic complementing the earthy, woodsy character of the maitake. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (~65 kcal/100g), low in carbohydrates (6.8 g/100g), and provides a meaningful amount of dietary fibre (2.5 g/100g), potassium (320 mg/100g), niacin (4.5 mg/100g), and riboflavin (0.22 mg/100g). The fat content (~4.5 g/100g) derives primarily from the cooking oil used in stir-frying. Maitake mushrooms are one of the few edible fungi that contain measurable vitamin D (1.2 mcg/100g) and are a recognised source of beta-glucan polysaccharides, including the D-fraction, which has been studied for immunomodulatory and potential blood-glucose-regulating effects. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making this dish appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Sodium content (~210 mg/100g) from soy sauce warrants attention for individuals with hypertension, though the high potassium content partially offsets this concern. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and cholesterol-free, and is suitable as a nutritious side dish at lunch or dinner in a balanced Chinese meal.
common_user_queries: Is maitake stir-fry good for diabetics?
How many calories in maitake mushroom stir-fry?
What are the health benefits of maitake mushrooms?
Is maitake stir-fry high in sodium?
What vitamins are in maitake mushrooms?
Can I eat maitake stir-fry if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of mushroom stir-fry?
What is Grifola frondosa used for in Chinese cooking?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw maitake mushroom data (FDC ID 169403) with documented adjustments for cooking oil, soy sauce sodium, and Shaoxing wine carbohydrate contribution. No exact match for this prepared dish exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy vegetables and mushrooms. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Mushrooms, maitake, raw (FDC ID 169403). Adjustments applied: fat increased to ~4.5 g/100g to account for stir-fry cooking oil; sodium set to 210 mg/100g to reflect light soy sauce addition; carbohydrate set to 6.8 g/100g incorporating minor contributions from soy sauce and Shaoxing wine; energy recalculated to ~65 kcal/100g. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100g, monounsaturated fat at 2.1 g/100g, and polyunsaturated fat at 1.4 g/100g based on typical vegetable oil fatty acid profile. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — maitake mushrooms are a plant-derived fungal food and contain no vitamin B12; this is naturally zero for all non-animal foods. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible — no hydrogenated oils used in traditional preparation. 3. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for the stir-fried preparation of maitake with garlic and soy sauce is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17. 4. GI estimated at 15 (low) using analogous estimate methodology from University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy mushrooms and vegetables. No direct GI measurement for maitake stir-fry exists in any approved GI source. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 6.8 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 1.5. 5. Halal status marked false due to presence of Shaoxing wine (alcohol); dish can be made halal by substituting with non-alcoholic rice vinegar or omitting wine. 6. Duplicate portion entry in input JSON corrected — single default serving of 150 g retained. 7. Input source 'International Journal of Molecular Sciences' is not on the approved source list and has not been used as a primary nutrient evidence source; USDA FoodData Central used instead. The IJMS reference is retained only as supporting context for health guidance claims regarding maitake bioactive compounds. 8. Ontology match types corrected to use only allowed values from the approved set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']: foodon_match_type set to 'closest_parent' (previously 'broader'); foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' (previously 'broader'). This resolves the G5 hard gate failure from iteration 2.
